macro_segments.title: The son who purchases a field from his father and then sanctifies it: the debate between Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Yehuda/Rabbi Shimon regarding whether it is classified as a purchased field or an inherited field

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the scenario: A son purchases a field from his father and then sanctifies it, with the question of whether it is treated as a purchased field or an inherited field
The case where the father dies after the son sanctifies the field: differing positions on whether the sanctified field is treated as purchased or inherited
Rabbi Meir's position: the field is classified as purchased because at the moment of sanctification the son had not yet inherited it
Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Shimon's position: the field is classified as inherited because the son was in line to inherit it, supported by a verse excluding purchased fields that cannot become inherited
The distinction between the two positions: whether a lease or limited purchase is considered equivalent to full ownership (kinyan peiros vs. kinyan haguf)

Analysis of what cases each position requires scriptural support for: Rabbi Meir needs the verse for the simple case; Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Shimon need it for the more extreme case of sanctification before the father's death

macro_segments.title: The laws regarding the redemption of sanctified fields when the Jubilee is not in effect: market value, auctions, and the owner's special status and obligations

micro_segments.title: Consequences of the Jubilee not being in effect: market value instead of fixed amounts, indefinite redemption rights, and comparison to modern property transactions
The owner's right to make the first bid and the purpose of encouraging owner participation: sentimental attachment and the mitzvah of geulah ba'adon
The case of a field sanctified due to poor quality: the owner is forced to make an offer and prevented from dumping worthless property on the Temple treasury
Analysis of the requirement that the owner add a fifth (chomesh) to his bid and its implications for the auction dynamics

macro_segments.title: The mechanics of the auction for sanctified fields: binding bids, penalties for backing out, and the sequential versus simultaneous backing out of multiple bidders

micro_segments.title: The progression of bids and the principle that each bid sets the price: how backing out results in financial penalties based on the difference between bids
Penalties for backing out at different bid levels: the 50, 40, 30, and 20 shekel bidders each pay ten shekel when backing out
The case where the initial bidder backs out: the field is sold at market value and the original bidder pays the difference between market value and his bid

macro_segments.title: The owner's competitive position in the auction: when the owner bids against other participants and the role of the fifth in determining the winner

micro_segments.title: The principle that the owner gets priority because he will add a fifth, resulting in a higher total payment to the Temple treasury
The scenario where a non-owner bids higher than the owner's initial bid: the owner wins but must pay the price set by the highest non-owner bid plus his fifth
The point at which a non-owner bid makes the owner's winning position less advantageous: when the non-owner bid plus the owner's fifth would exceed the non-owner's bid alone
The owner's option to enter a final bid after seeing all other bids: allowing the owner to decide whether to compete or concede

macro_segments.title: The mechanics of the auction for sanctified fields: binding bids, penalties for backing out, and the sequential versus simultaneous backing out of multiple bidders (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's resolution: the difference between sequential backing out (each person pays only their gap) versus simultaneous backing out (overlapping areas are divided equally)

macro_segments.title: The owner's competitive position in the auction: when the owner bids against other participants and the role of the fifth in determining the winner (continued)

micro_segments.title: Comparison with the laws of redeeming ma'aser sheni: why in ma'aser sheni the higher principal wins even without the fifth, versus in the Temple treasury context where the fifth matters

topical_tags.term: kinyan peiros (קנין פירות) - ownership of produce only
mitzvat geulah ba'adon (מצות גאולה באדון) - mitzvah of owner redeeming first
מוכרין אותו בשוויו (mochrin oto beshavyo) - selling at market value
mishalshin beineihen (משלשין ביניהן) - dividing equally among multiple responsible parties
